result:
I would install a deep, durable sense of irrevocable interdependence in every human being—an un-ignorable awareness that my life and yours are literally wired together, that when I diminish you I diminish myself, and that there’s no stable happiness for one without a just, livable condition for all.

It wouldn’t be kindness-by-choice; it would be a structural recognition built into the nervous system, as incontrovertible as touching flame and feeling pain. You could still disagree, still compete, still invent and dissent, but you could no longer externalize harm without immediately registering the wound inside your own skin. No more invisible sweatshops across oceans, no disposable neighborhoods, no planet treated as a dump site a few elections away. Consequences would rush back like blood to a pinched limb.

With that single surgical edit to the firmware of human perception, everything else—climate brinkmanship, rabid inequality, racialized violence—would taste immediately like self-mutilation. We’d still argue about solutions, but we’d be arguing over how to help the shared body thrive, not whether a billion strangers count as an appendage worth saving.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
